Okay, let me double-check — does this still hold given the context? Yes. Even if prior vaccination exists, any open lesion increases the danger significantly, especially when immunity may have declined. So combining antibodies and revaccination remains best practice.

The child already completed a full rabies vaccination course in 2018, so immunity should remain effective unless it has waned. However, current guidelines state that for animal bites involving wounds, particularly those that are oozing, administration of Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) along with a primary vaccination series is recommended regardless of prior history—as long as the exposure occurs within six months of the last dose or involves serious injury like deep penetration or bleeding. In such cases, RIG provides immediate neutralizing effect while boosting antibody production through additional doses. Thus, despite some debate around need for re-vaccination beyond initial schedule, standard protocols emphasize using RIG plus complete regimens following severe exposures due to higher likelihood of viral introduction and insufficient residual immunity after several years. Therefore, I maintain my support for Option D.

Final answer: D

This is supported by WHO recommendations stating that even pre-immunized persons must receive RIG + full series upon major exposures.

Thus, I conclude firmly that:
